title: Graph Convolutional Network
body:
# GCN (Why?)

As part of a course I am doing for my Masters at Macquarie Uni, I was introduced to Graph Neural Networks. Understanding why they exist is really interesting and the reason is quite simple, traditional neural networks take advantage of highly structured data. An example of this is an image, an image is basically just a grid of pixels right? It's highly structured and follows the same format always so mapping it to a neural network is no problem.

So what happens when we try to give complex relationships to a neural network? Like consider social media, we can easily map this out as a graph where each user is a node and the relationship between the users are the edges. Now this is highly unstructured and dynamic (there is no fixed format). You could theoretically make the argument of flattening this out into an adjacency matrix and passing that to a neural network. But that brings out a fundamental flaw, that again Neural Networks consider the ordering of the data. Now for a graph relationship you can move the nodes around but as long as the connection remains the same it doesn't matter conceptually. But to a neural network, feeding two conceptually same graphs is treated as two different inputs, giving two different outputs...

In simpler terms those two conceptually same graphs will have two different adjacency matrices (even-though they mean the same). So this brings us to a very simple yet interesting concept called "Permutation Invariance". Permutations as you may know just refers to the ordering of the elements and invariance means it shouldn't matter to the algorithm. So we need a way to map the relationships in a way that achieves Permutation Invariance.

And here enters Graph Convolutional Networks. Again I know this sounds annoying but the idea here is very simple. Instead of looking at the entire adjacency matrix, GCNs look at the graph in a local lens. So for each node it just looks at its local neighbours to extract and aggregate information using neural networks (generating local embeddings). 

To understand the approach better, what's happening are two things. To figure out the embedding of any target node: Aggregate information from its local neighbours (Average)  -> Pass onto a neural network. That's it! This approach is Permutation Invariant as now the ordering of the nodes does not matter, so no matter how you shuffle the nodes, the concepts and meanings are always captured using local embeddings.

P.S there is a looot of math behind this but honestly I am more interested in the concepts :)
